title: Shed Chicken Coop (Walk-In)
summary: A full walk-in shed coop: framed floor on piers, four stud walls, a 15° shed roof, T1-11 siding, shuttered windows, and an external nesting box you gather eggs from without going in.
description:
This is the coop for people done with crouching: a real 8x4-footprint stick-framed shed you walk into upright, with a human door, two shuttered windows, and the flock-keeper's favorite feature — an external nesting box hung on the wall, with its own hinged lid, so morning eggs happen without entering the coop at all.

It's genuine small-building construction and a better first shed than a shed, because chickens don't complain about learning curves. Floor system on pier blocks with treated 4x4s (foundation practice varies by region — the source says ask locally, and that's the right answer), 2x4 walls framed flat and tipped up, a 15° single-slope roof built into the back wall's angled studs, T1-11 siding, three sheets of 1/2" ply and roofing on top.

The details that make it a COOP rather than a shed: hardware cloth or chicken wire stapled inside the window openings (shutters close over them in weather — ventilation with predator security), the nesting box built from a 1x12 with a sloped lid so hens don't roost on it, and linoleum over the plywood floor, which turns the worst job in chicken-keeping into a scrape-and-hose job.

The cut list covers the framing; trim is measure-to-fit because T1-11 thickness varies (the source declines to fake those numbers, and so does this plan). One framing subtlety in step 4: the back wall's top plate must NOT overhang its studs — the T1-11 sheathes flush all the way up the back face.

Predator note, because it's the difference between a coop and a buffet: staple hardware cloth, not just chicken wire, anywhere raccoons can reach. Chicken wire keeps chickens in; it does not keep clever hands out.

tools.note: Faster for the repeated stud cuts; the circular saw covers everything.
16d framing and 2" galvanized nails — or screw everything if you'd rather.
1-1/2" pocket holes are the easy way to hang rafters; toenailing and brackets also work.
The floor on its piers, then every wall. A coop out of level telegraphs into every door and shutter.
Hardware cloth into the window openings.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet, flooring grade
materials.note: The floor. Cover with linoleum — future-you, on cleaning day, insists.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft or stud length
materials.note: Only 5 must be true 8-footers; the rest can be studs.

materials.name: Hinges, assorted
materials.note: Door, four shutters, nesting box lid.

materials.name: Pocket hole screws, 1-1/2" and 1-1/4"
materials.note: Rafters (with 2-1/2" screws), doors, and nesting box.

materials.name: Roofing, ~50 sq ft
materials.note: Metal panels or shingles, your call and your climate's.

materials.name: Hardware cloth and staples
materials.note: Windows. Hardware cloth stops raccoons; chicken wire only stops chickens.

cutList.note: Four per wall — build two walls.
Top end cut at 15° — the roof slope lives in these studs.
Both ends 15°, parallel, then the bottom point trimmed square 2-5/8" down — see the step; it gives a flush face for the front wall.
One end at 30° — the sloped lid line. Hens don't roost on a slope, which is the point.

steps.title: Foundation, then floor
steps.body:
Sort the foundation first — pier blocks with treated 4x4s on top is the simple default, but frost depth and drainage vary, so ask locally (the source's advice, and correct).

Frame the floor 24" on center with 16d nails or 3" screws, square it by diagonals, and sheath with 3/4" ply glued with subfloor adhesive and ring-shank nailed. Cold climate? Slip rigid foam between the joists now. Set the floor on the foundation and shim dead level — every wall inherits this surface. Then lay linoleum over the plywood; it converts cleaning day from scraping to hosing.

steps.title: Frame the front wall
steps.body: On a big flat area, frame the front wall: plates, studs, and the door opening with its jack studs and header. Nail or screw, then square by diagonals before it leaves the ground.

steps.title: Frame the side walls
steps.body: Two side walls, each with a framed window (headers and sills from the 21-1/2" pieces). The windows can move or resize freely — they're ventilation, and the shutters will be built to whatever you frame. Use scrap to frame the nesting-box opening in one side wall, with a lip to help hens step in.

steps.title: Frame the back wall — plate flush
steps.body: The back studs are cut at 15° on top (84-1/2" long point) so the roof slope is built into the wall. Fasten the top plate WITHOUT letting it overhang the studs — the T1-11 runs flush all the way up the back face, and a proud plate would hold the siding off the framing.

steps.title: Stand the walls, hang the rafters
steps.body: Stand and brace the walls, screwing down into the floor joists and to each other, checking plumb as you go. Then the rafters: 2x4s cut 15° both ends (parallel), with the bottom point trimmed off square 2-5/8" down — that flat lets each rafter land flush on the front wall. Hang them with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws, or toenail, or use brackets; all three work, the pocket holes are just the least cursing.

steps.title: Side, trim, roof
steps.body:
Hang the T1-11 in full sheets, trimming only length. Trim front and back first with full 8-foot 1x4s top and bottom, then the sides — all trim cut to fit, because T1-11 thickness varies and the source rightly refuses to pretend otherwise. 2" galvanized nails throughout.

Roof: three sheets of 1/2" ply (center piece gets one lengthwise cut — the 24" o.c. studs make the math work), nailed off, then your roofing over it.

steps.title: Build and hang the nesting box
steps.body:
Build the box from the 1x12: 30°-sloped sides, bottom, front trim, 2x4 back supports. Screw it to the side wall THROUGH the studs at the framed opening, and hinge the sloped lid.

The slope isn't style — a flat nesting box top becomes a roost, and a roost above the nest means fouled eggs. The outside lid means eggs without entering: the feature you'll use 365 mornings a year.

steps.title: Windows, shutters, wire
steps.body: Trim the window openings in 1x3, staple HARDWARE CLOTH over them from inside (raccoons open chicken wire like a lunchbox), and build the four shutters — three 1x4 boards battened with 1x2s each — hung on hinges so they close over the wire in rain or cold.

steps.title: The people door
steps.body:
Frame the door in 1x4s with 1-1/4" pocket holes, back it with leftover ply or 1x6s, and build it to YOUR opening with 1/8" gaps all around. Hang it, add 1x2 door-stop moulding inside the jamb (stop and weather seal in one), and latch it — with the string-through-the-wall trick if the latch is chicken-height.

Move the flock in. You'll stand upright in there tomorrow morning and wonder why anyone builds the crouching kind.

images.alt: Walk-in shed style chicken coop with shuttered windows and external nesting box
